does: This paper proposes AI Alignment Dialogues as an interactive method for achieving alignment between users and support agents through ongoing conversation rather than learning from behavioral data alone. The core contribution is introducing a dialogue-based framework where users and agents collaboratively establish and maintain alignment through direct interaction, specifically designed for behavior support agents that help users achieve desired future goals. The authors outline the conceptual structure of alignment dialogues, present a qualitative focus group study to understand user impacts, and develop a model describing how such dialogues influence user engagement and trust with the agent system.
connects: This work builds on the foundation of AI alignment research that traditionally relies on inverse reinforcement learning and preference learning from behavioral datasets, responding to limitations of purely data-driven approaches. It connects to recent work on interactive machine learning and human-in-the-loop AI systems that emphasize bidirectional communication over one-way data extraction. The paper also relates to transparency and explainability research in AI, drawing on work showing that direct communication of agent reasoning increases user trust. Additionally, it extends behavior change support system literature, combining insights from behavioral science about goal-setting and motivation with AI safety principles to create agent systems that support human aspirations rather than merely reproduce current behavior.
enables: This interactive framework opens pathways for more transparent and trustworthy AI agents in consumer and healthcare applications where maintaining user agency is critical. By enabling direct transmission of high-level values and goals through dialogue, the approach supports future work on value alignment that doesn't require large behavioral datasets or inverse reinforcement learning. The dialogue-based methodology could extend to more complex multi-stakeholder alignment scenarios where different parties must negotiate system behavior. This work also enables research into agent design principles that support human autonomy and deliberation, potentially informing development of AI systems that serve as collaborative partners rather than behavioral predictors, particularly valuable for support agents helping users navigate behavior change or decision-making under uncertainty.
